title: Roast leg of lamb
subtitle: Garlic-studded, rosemary-scented, served pink.
excerpt: A whole leg studded with garlic and rosemary, started hot and finished slow until the centre reads 60°C for a blushing pink. The Sunday roast that takes the least fuss and rewards patience at the carving board.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — roast leg of mutton chapter gives the baseline temperature and resting doctrine. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg (the garlic-studded technique and the mint-sauce companion). Traditional British Sunday-roast practice documented across twentieth-century British cookery columns.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stud the leg with garlic and rosemary the evening before and leave uncovered on a rack in the fridge overnight. The surface dries and browns faster in the oven. Bring back to room temperature one hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: start hot, then drop to 180°C fan for the slow roast

content.text: A whole leg of lamb at 2.2 kg feeds six comfortably, and the only real decision is how pink you want it. A centre temperature of 60°C gives a rosy interior that holds its colour on the board; 65°C runs to pale pink, the kind that passes muster with more cautious tables. Start the oven screaming hot to set a brown crust, then drop the heat and let the lamb coast to temperature. A thermometer is not optional.

Garlic and rosemary are the traditional companions because both are assertive enough to hold their own against the mineral sweetness of the meat. Cut the pockets deep enough that the garlic disappears inside the muscle, not just under the surface fat. Studded three to four hours ahead the flavour barely registers; studded overnight it runs right through.
What you need

A roasting rack lifts the leg above the tray vegetables so the underside browns and the fat renders downwards. An instant-read thermometer is the whole recipe; guessing by time alone produces variable results. Have a large, heavy roasting pan — the vegetables underneath take a lot of heat.
Method
Stud the leg

Take the leg out of the fridge one hour before cooking. Make 18 deep incisions across the surface of the meat with the point of a small knife: push {{garlic}} of garlic slivers and small pieces of {{rosemary}} rosemary sprigs into each pocket. Rub the joint all over with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, then season generously with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes and {{black-pepper}} of cracked black pepper.
Build the tray and start hot

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Scatter {{onion}} halved onions and {{carrot}} roughly chopped carrots across the base of the roasting pan. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot chicken stock. Sit the rack over the vegetables and set the leg on the rack. Roast at 220°C for 20 minutes to set a crust, then drop the heat to 200°C (180°C fan).
Roast to temperature

Continue roasting at 200°C for a further 55 to 70 minutes. Start probing at 50 minutes: push the thermometer into the thickest part of the muscle, away from the bone. The reading at the bone is always higher than the true centre. Pull the leg at 58°C for pink, 62°C for pale pink. The leg carries on cooking in the rest.
Rest the lamb

Transfer the leg to a warm plate, tent loosely with foil, and rest for at least 30 minutes. A rested leg holds its juices; a carved-too-soon leg bleeds pink across the board. Use the resting time to strain the tray juices for gravy.
Make the pan gravy

Tip the tray contents into a sieve set over a small saucepan and press well. Skim the fat from the surface of the strained liquid, then simmer over medium heat until reduced by a third, around five minutes. Season and pour into a warm jug. This is a clean, lightly enriched roasting jus rather than a thickened gravy — it suits the lamb better.
Carve and serve

Carve by angling the knife across the top of the bone, slicing down in long strokes to produce even rounds. Once the easy meat is gone, hold the bone end and work around it. The meat near the shank end is darker and more strongly flavoured. Serve the lighter leg meat first, the shank meat alongside.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Timing by weight rather than temperature is the most common failure.

items.cause: No thermometer used, or pulled too late. The leg continues to cook after it leaves the oven.
items.fix: Use an instant-read thermometer and pull at 58°C. Rest loosely tented, not tightly wrapped.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough at the start, or leg still cold from the fridge.
items.fix: Rest the leg at room temperature for a full hour. Start at a full 220°C for 20 minutes before dropping the temperature.

items.cause: Pockets not cut deep enough, or studded just before cooking.
items.fix: Push the slivers into the muscle with the blade of a small knife. Do it at least two hours ahead.
A 2.2 kg leg needs a full 30 minutes under a loose foil tent. Tightly wrapped it steams and the crust softens.

items.symptom: Meat near the bone still pink-red (raw) while outer meat is done
items.cause: The bone conducts heat erratically. The thermometer was placed too close to the bone.
items.fix: Always probe the thickest part of the muscle, well clear of the bone. The bone area reads hotter than the true centre.

content.text: Make ahead and leftovers

Stud the leg the evening before and refrigerate uncovered. On the day, bring to room temperature before roasting. Cold leftover lamb is excellent in sandwiches with mint sauce and thinly sliced red onion. Shredded into a pan with leftover roasting juices and served on thick toast, it holds another meal. Lamb bone makes a good stock for a Middle-Eastern style soup: cover with cold water, simmer three hours, add chickpeas, cumin, and lemon.
Where this dish lives

Roast leg of lamb has been the British Easter and Sunday-roast centrepiece since lamb first arrived on English tables in any quantity. Mrs Beeton's 1861 book records it as one of the cheaper roasting joints compared to beef, suited to the smaller household with a modest oven. The combination of garlic, rosemary, and lamb arrived in British domestic cooking through French influence in the nineteenth century, previously the English preference was for mint sauce only. It sits best alongside roast potatoes, mint sauce, peas, and a light gravy — the full Sunday roast plate where the lamb takes the centre.
